title: Apple Crumble
subtitle: Bramley apples under a butter, oat, and flour crumble topping
excerpt: Bramley apples cut into thick pieces and sweetened, covered with a crumble of flour, oats, butter, and demerara sugar, then baked until the fruit is soft and the topping is golden and rubble-textured. Served with custard or cream.
sourceNotes: Apple crumble became a British household staple during the Second World War when rationing made pastry difficult. The crumble topping used less fat than a pie crust and produced a reliable result with minimal equipment. It has remained one of the most widely made British desserts since.

content.text: Bramley apples are the right choice for crumble. They collapse readily when cooked, releasing liquid into the dish that bubbles up through the edges of the crumble during baking and gives the topping its characteristic bottom crust. Eating apples hold their shape better and give a drier, firmer filling, not wrong, but different. Use whatever you have, but expect a different result.

The crumble topping needs to go onto the fruit cold. Warm butter will melt before the crumble reaches the oven and give a sandy, oily topping rather than a rubble one. Work quickly after rubbing in the butter and get the dish into the oven while the topping is still cold from your hands.

items.name: Bramley apple
items.prepNote: peeled, cored, and cut into 3 cm pieces (about 4 large apples)

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Toss {{apple-bramley}} of prepared Bramley apple with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and the juice of {{lemon}}. Tip into a 1.5-litre baking dish and spread level.

Combine {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{rolled-oats}} of rolled oats, and {{demerara-sugar}} of demerara sugar in a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter cubes and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse, uneven crumbs with some pea-sized lumps remaining. The lumps will give the topping texture. Do not overwork it into a fine powder.

Scatter the crumble evenly over the apple filling. Do not press it down. Bake for 35, 40 minutes until the topping is golden and the fruit juices are bubbling up at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving with custard, cream, or vanilla ice cream.

items.fix: Raise to 200°C fan for the last 10 minutes. The topping should be no deeper than about 1.5 cm. Make sure the demerara sugar is included, it gives colour and crunch.
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or the topping was packed too thick
items.symptom: Crumble topping is pale and soft rather than golden

items.fix: Toss the apple in 1 tbsp of plain flour before adding to the dish. This thickens the juices during baking.
items.cause: Too much liquid released from the apples without a flour-based filling
items.symptom: Filling is watery and hasn't thickened

items.fix: Use butter straight from the fridge. Work quickly and keep the crumble cold before it goes into the oven.
items.cause: Butter was melted or too soft when rubbed in
items.symptom: Crumble has turned to a hard, solid crust

items.fix: Cut the apple into pieces no larger than 3 cm. If still firm after the crumble is golden, cover loosely with foil and bake for a further 10 minutes.
items.cause: Bramley apple was cut too thick, or oven temperature was too low
items.symptom: Apple is still firm after baking

content.text: Apple crumble became a British kitchen staple during the Second World War. Butter was rationed, so the crumble topping used less fat than a pastry crust. It turned out to be a very good pudding on its own. Today it sits in the front rank of British puddings. The same topping works over many other fruits, from rhubarb to plum to blackberry.
